metadata.summary: Laravel backend slice architecture: Controller → FormRequest → Service → Eloquent → Resource. Thin controllers, business logic in services, validation in Form Requests, explicit API responses.

content.title: PHP Laravel Backend
content.summary: Production Laravel backend patterns: Controller → FormRequest → Service → Eloquent model → API Resource slice. Controllers are thin HTTP adapters (10-30 lines). Business logic and DB::transaction live in service classes. Form Requests own validation and policy authorization. API Resources produce explicit field lists. No Request::all(), no Eloquent in controllers, no request() in services.

why.p: Laravel's batteries (auth, queues, mail, broadcasting, scheduler) accelerate CRUD-heavy backends, but the framework's permissiveness allows fat controllers and mass-assignment vulnerabilities by default. The full-slice approach enforces separation of concerns at every layer, makes each layer testable in isolation, and produces consistent API contracts via Resources regardless of underlying Eloquent changes.

list.item: Greenfield CRUD-heavy backends (SaaS, e-commerce, internal tools) with 1-5 person teams.
API-first products pairing Sanctum/Passport for auth and Resource classes for serialization.
Brownfield migration from raw PHP, WordPress, or CodeIgniter when shipping speed matters.
Any project where convention-over-configuration can replace bespoke framework glue.
Sub-millisecond hot paths (HFT, ad serving) — PHP request bootstrap (~30-80ms cold) is a floor.
Heavy CPU/ML workloads — use Laravel as the front door, offload via queues.
WebSocket-first apps with persistent connections — use Reverb only as a complement.
Stateful long-lived processes (game servers, media transcoders) — wrong runtime model.
Teams with zero PHP experience facing a hard deadline.

rule.statement: Each new resource requires exactly: Eloquent model + migration, FormRequest (store + update), Service, Controller, API Resource + ResourceCollection, Policy, and a feature test. Omitting any layer creates a gap that agents will fill incorrectly on the next iteration.
rule.rationale: Partial slices accumulate technical debt faster than full-slice creation. A missing Policy is an authorization hole; a missing FormRequest exposes validation gaps; a missing Resource leaks internal model fields.

rule.statement: Use constructor injection with typed parameters (private readonly UserService $userService) in controllers and services. Never instantiate dependencies with new inside class methods.
rule.rationale: Typed constructor injection makes dependencies visible to the container, enables mocking in tests, and prevents service-locator antipattern (App::make inside methods).

rule.statement: Wrap all multi-step writes in DB::transaction(fn () => ...). Place queue dispatch and external calls after the transaction with ->afterCommit() or equivalent. Never put external HTTP inside a transaction.
rule.rationale: DB transactions hold locks. External HTTP calls inside a transaction can lock rows for the duration of a network timeout, causing deadlocks across concurrent requests on the same rows.

rule.statement: Use FormRequest::validated() as the sole source of data for creates and updates. Never pass $request->all() or $request->input() raw into service methods or model creates.
rule.rationale: $request->all() includes all request keys including those not in $fillable, enabling mass-assignment of protected fields. validated() returns only keys defined in rules().

rule.statement: Register all API routes under Route::apiResource() with versioned namespaces (Api\V1\) and explicit middleware: auth:sanctum and throttle. Never add routes to the CSRF except list for API endpoints — use token authentication exclusively.
rule.rationale: Routes without auth middleware are publicly accessible. CSRF exceptions for API routes are never needed when token auth is the only entry point.

example.note: apiResource registers index, store, show, update, destroy automatically. Add ->only() or ->except() to restrict.

rule.statement: Always define $fillable explicitly — never use $guarded = []. Define $casts for all non-string attributes (datetime, boolean, array/JSON). Hide $hidden fields (password, remember_token) at the model level.
rule.rationale: $guarded = [] allows any column to be mass-assigned. Explicit $fillable and $casts are the first defense against property injection and datetime format inconsistencies.

rule.statement: Define Eloquent scopes for reusable query constraints (scopeActive, scopeSearch, scopeWithRole). Call scopes from the repository, not from controllers or services directly.
rule.rationale: Scopes encapsulate query logic with the model. Repeating WHERE clauses across service methods produces inconsistent behavior when the business rule changes.

example.note: Accessor/mutator for email: use Attribute::make(set: fn($v) => strtolower($v)) to normalize on write, not on read.

rule.statement: Add a Repository class only when there is a real second backing implementation (e.g., Postgres primary + ElasticSearch search). A UserRepository that delegates all calls to User::query() adds indirection with no value — Eloquent is already a repository.
rule.rationale: An unnecessary repository layer means two places to change for every schema update: the model and the repository. Agents emit it by default from older tutorials.

rule.statement: When a repository exists, eager-load all relations needed by the caller: $this->model->with(['roles', 'orders'])->findOrFail($id). Never rely on lazy loading from inside a Resource.
rule.rationale: Resources that lazy-load relations produce N+1 queries on list endpoints. The query count scales with page size.

antipattern.description: $request->all() passed directly to Model::create() or a service method without going through FormRequest::validated().
antipattern.reason: $request->all() includes every key the client sends — including is_admin, role_id, or other protected columns. If those keys are in $fillable, the client has elevated its own privileges.

antipattern.description: Eloquent::find($id) returning null without findOrFail(), with the controller returning a 200 response with null data.
antipattern.reason: Clients receive a 200 with an empty or null body and must guess whether the record exists. findOrFail() triggers a ModelNotFoundException that maps to 404 via the exception handler.

antipattern.description: $guarded = [] on a model with many columns, relying on FormRequest to prevent mass-assignment.
antipattern.reason: Future model columns added without a matching FormRequest update are immediately writable by any authenticated client. Defense-in-depth requires both $fillable and FormRequest validation.

antipattern.description: new ConcreteClass() inside service or controller methods instead of constructor injection.
antipattern.reason: Directly instantiated dependencies cannot be swapped in tests. Container-managed dependencies support mocking, decoration, and lifetime management (scoped, singleton, transient).

antipattern.description: RefreshDatabase in every test class when tests are read-heavy, causing migrations to run once per test class and dominating CI time.
antipattern.reason: RefreshDatabase recreates the schema between test classes. DatabaseTransactions wraps each test in a rolled-back transaction — much faster for read-only or SELECT-then-assert patterns.

antipattern.description: APP_DEBUG=true or APP_KEY= or DB_PASSWORD=root shipped in .env files committed to version control.
antipattern.reason: Debug mode exposes stack traces and environment variables in HTTP responses. Empty APP_KEY breaks encryption. Committed credentials reach every developer's machine and CI logs.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive Laravel features with a subagent that owns the full slice: route → controller → form request → service → repository → Eloquent model + migration → API resource → feature test. The agent must run php artisan migrate:fresh --seed && php artisan test as a quality gate and vendor/bin/pint --test + vendor/bin/phpstan analyse to catch style/type drift. For larger features, brainstorm first (faion-brainstorm), then execute slice-by-slice.

list.item: composer — Dependency manager (https://getcomposer.org)
php artisan — Generators, migrate, test runner, tinker, route:list (Built-in)
vendor/bin/pint — Code style, Laravel-flavored PHP-CS-Fixer (composer require --dev laravel/pint)
vendor/bin/phpstan — Static analysis (composer require --dev nunomaduro/larastan)
vendor/bin/phpunit / php artisan test — Test runner (Built-in)
php artisan ide-helper:generate — Facade/Eloquent autocompletion stubs (barryvdh/laravel-ide-helper)
php artisan route:list — Inspect registered routes, vital before agent commits (Built-in)
php artisan tinker — REPL, ideal for agent verification steps (Built-in)
php artisan migrate:fresh --seed — Reset DB to known state (Built-in)
Treat controllers as thin HTTP adapters (10-30 lines). Push branching/validation into Form Requests, business logic into services.
Use Form Requests (StoreXRequest/UpdateXRequest) for all writes — never call $request->all() raw.
Wrap multi-step writes in DB::transaction(fn () => …). Pair with DB::afterCommit() for queued jobs to avoid race conditions.
Use Eloquent API Resources for output — never return models directly (they leak hidden fields, dates in wrong formats).
Adopt UUIDv7/ULID primary keys early for distributed systems; Eloquent supports them via HasUlids/HasUuids traits.
Cache routes + config in production (php artisan optimize), invalidate during deploy.
Pin PHP version via composer.json "php": ">=8.3" and CI matrix.
Run Larastan at level 8 and treat new violations as build failures.

template.purpose: CRUD controller with typed constructor injection, all five methods, Resource returns
Service with paginate, findOrFail, create (hash+event), update, delete
Eloquent model with fillable, casts, relationships, scopes, accessors
UserRepository with eager loading, pagination, findByEmail
